phase1.explanation:
Let's break this down line by line:

Line 1: public class HelloWorld — every Java program has a class. The class name MUST match the filename (HelloWorld.java).

Line 2: public static void main(String[] args) — the main method is where the program starts. Every Java app needs this exact signature.

Line 3: System.out.println("Hello, World!") — prints text to the console. System.out is the standard output, println prints with a new line at the end.

Line 4: } — closes the main method
Line 5: } — closes the class

Pro tip: Java is case-sensitive! 'String' not 'string'. Every statement ends with a semicolon ;
phase1.openingDialogue: Ah, the classic! Let me show you your very first Java program~ ✨

phase2.errorMessage:
HelloWorld.java:3: error: ';' expected
        System.out.println("Hello, World!")
                                             ^
1 error
phase2.explanation:
See the missing semicolon at the end of line 3? In Java, EVERY statement must end with a semicolon (;). The compiler is pointing right at where it expected the semicolon — the error message even shows a ^ mark!

This is the #1 mistake beginners make. Always check your semicolons!

phase3.prompt:
Write a Java program that prints "Hello, Java-chan!" to the console.

Requirements:
• Use a class named HelloJava
• Use the correct main method signature
• Print exactly: Hello, Java-chan!
dialogueHints: The class name must be HelloJava — and it must match the filename!
Make sure you have the exact text 'Hello, Java-chan!' inside quotes.
Don't forget the semicolon at the end of the println statement!
phase3.openingDialogue: Your turn! Let's see what you've got~ 💪
